title: Core Java Basics
overview: Java is a general-purpose, object-oriented programming language.
It compiles to bytecode instead of native machine code.
That one design choice is why the same program runs unchanged on almost any operating system.
why: A backend engineer needs to know not just how to write Java, but why it behaves the way it does.
Interviewers often start with these fundamentals before going deeper.
Understanding the "why" makes every later topic — JVM, memory, concurrency — easier to reason about.
problem: Before Java, a C or C++ program compiled straight to native machine code for one specific operating system.
The same program had to be recompiled separately for Windows, for Linux, and for every other target.
Manual memory management (`malloc`/`free`) was a common source of leaks and crashes.
James Gosling and his team at Sun Microsystems set out to fix both problems at once, in 1995.

intro: Java's core identity comes down to three things: its features, where it's used, and how it starts running.

topics.heading: Key Features
points: **Platform Independent** — via bytecode + the JVM.
**Object-Oriented** — everything except primitives is modeled as an object.
**Simple & Secure** — no explicit pointers, automatic garbage collection.
**Robust** — strong compile-time type checking, mandatory handling for checked exceptions.
**Multithreaded** — built-in support for concurrent execution.
**High Performance** — a JIT compiler converts hot bytecode to native machine code at runtime.

topics.heading: Where Java Is Used Today
points: Enterprise backend systems.
Android apps.
Large-scale distributed and microservices systems.
Big data tools — Hadoop, Kafka, and Spark all run on the JVM.
Banking and fintech systems.

topics.heading: How the JVM Calls main()
points: The required signature is exactly `public static void main(String[] args)`.
Every keyword in it exists for a reason, not by convention.
**`public`** — the JVM sits outside your class, so it needs access from anywhere.
**`static`** — the JVM calls `main` before any object of the class exists.
**`void`** — the program signals a result via `System.exit(int code)`, not a return value.
**How it's invoked** — the JVM uses reflection to find and call this exact method: roughly `MyApp.class.getMethod("main", String[].class).invoke(null, args)`.
**A wrong signature fails immediately** — `Error: Main method not found in class MyApp`, before any of your code runs.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["HelloWorld.java"] --> B["javac compiles once"]
    B --> C["HelloWorld.class - bytecode"]
    C --> D["JVM on Windows"]
    C --> E["JVM on Linux"]
    C --> F["JVM on Mac"]
architectureFlow.caption: One compiled file. Three different operating systems. No recompiling.

simpleExample.scenario: The exact entry point signature the JVM looks for.
simpleExample.input: Running `java HelloWorld` from the command line, with no extra arguments.
simpleExample.execution: The JVM loads `HelloWorld.class`, uses reflection to find `public static void main(String[] args)`, and invokes it with an empty `args` array.
simpleExample.explanation: If any part of that signature were different — not `public`, not `static`, wrong return type — the JVM would fail before this line ever printed.

realWorldExample.scenario: A command-line tool that processes a batch of orders for one merchant, in a given environment.
realWorldExample.execution: The JVM populates `args` as `{"4521", "prod"}`, and the program reads `args[0]` and `args[1]`.
realWorldExample.output: The batch processor runs against merchant `4521` in the `prod` environment.
realWorldExample.explanation: `args.length < 2` guards against a missing argument, and `System.exit(1)` signals failure to the OS with a non-zero exit code — `main` itself can't return a value directly.

comparisonTables.title: Java vs C/C++ (Portability)
rows: Runs unchanged, if a JVM exists
Rare — GC and no raw pointers

bestPractices: Understand WORA (bytecode + JVM) before diving into deeper topics — most later concepts build on it.
Always check `args.length` before indexing into `args[]` in a command-line tool.
Use `System.exit(code)` deliberately, with a non-zero code, to signal failure to scripts calling your program.
commonMistakes: Assuming Java compiles directly to machine code like C or C++ — it compiles to bytecode instead.
Forgetting that `public static void main(String[] args)` must match exactly, or the JVM won't find it.
Indexing into `args[]` without checking `args.length` first, causing an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
interviewTips: If asked why Java is popular, don't just say "it's platform independent" — explain that bytecode + JVM is the mechanism that makes it true.
Be ready to state the exact `main` method signature and explain each keyword's purpose individually.

faq.q: Is Java fully platform-independent?
faq.a: The bytecode is platform-independent — the same .class file runs on any OS. The JVM itself is platform-specific; you need a JVM built for each OS, but that's usually already provided wherever Java is installed.

faq.q: Why does the main method have to be static?
faq.a: Because the JVM calls main before it creates any instance of your class. A non-static method requires an object to call it on, and no object exists yet at that point.

faq.q: Can I overload the main method?
faq.a: Yes, but the JVM only ever calls the exact public static void main(String[] args) signature as the entry point. Any overloads are just normal methods you'd have to call yourself.

summary: Java compiles to bytecode, not native machine code — that's the mechanism behind "write once, run anywhere".
It was created to solve two specific problems: platform portability and manual memory management.
The JVM finds and calls `public static void main(String[] args)` via reflection as the fixed entry point of every Java program.
quickRevision: James Gosling created Java at Sun Microsystems, released in 1995.
WORA: Java compiles to bytecode, and the JVM runs that same bytecode on any OS.
Java fixed two problems from C/C++: platform portability and manual memory management.
Java is used in enterprise backends, Android, big data, and banking systems.
The exact entry point signature: `public static void main(String[] args)`.
`public` — callable from outside the class. `static` — no instance exists yet. `void` — use `System.exit()` instead of a return value.
The JVM finds `main()` via reflection.
A wrong `main()` signature fails immediately with "Main method not found", before any code runs.

beginner: Who created Java, and when?
What does WORA stand for, and what does it mean?
What is the exact signature of Java's main method?
intermediate: Why must `main` be static?
What two specific problems did Java solve compared to C/C++?
What happens if the main method's signature is even slightly wrong?
advanced: How does the JVM actually locate and invoke `main()` internally?
Can you have multiple overloaded `main` methods in one class? What would happen?
Explain exactly why bytecode makes Java platform-independent, down to the JVM's role.

title: JDK, JRE & JVM
overview: JDK, JRE, and JVM are three related but different things.
Each one is a bigger package containing the one before it.
This page also covers how a class actually gets loaded and executed inside the JVM.
why: Every Java interview eventually asks for the difference between these three.
Knowing just the definitions isn't enough — you need to know what each one is actually for.
It also explains why building Java code needs a JDK, but running it only needs a JRE.
problem: A single "Java" installation could mean three very different things.
Someone who only wants to run a Java app doesn't need a compiler.
Someone who wants to write Java code needs a lot more than just an execution engine.
Java splits these needs into three nested packages, instead of one all-or-nothing install.

intro: Three ideas make up this topic: the three packages, how a class gets loaded, and how it actually runs.

topics.heading: JDK vs JRE vs JVM
topics.imageAlt: Diagram showing JDK containing JRE and dev tools, JRE containing the JVM, and the flow from .java source through javac, bytecode, ClassLoader, and the Execution Engine to native code
points: **JVM** — the engine that loads bytecode and converts it into machine code the CPU can run.
Platform-specific — a Windows JVM differs from a Linux JVM, but both accept identical bytecode.
**JRE** — JVM plus the core class libraries (`java.lang`, `java.util`) needed to run a program. No compiler.
**JDK** — JRE plus development tools: `javac`, `jdb`, `javadoc`. Needed to develop Java programs.

topics.heading: ClassLoader Subsystem
points: Loads `.class` files in three phases: **Loading**, **Linking**, **Initialization**.
**Bootstrap ClassLoader** — loads core JDK classes. Native code, has no parent.
**Platform/Extension ClassLoader** — loads classes from the JDK's extension libraries.
**Application ClassLoader** — loads classes from the application's own classpath.
**Delegation model** — a class loader asks its parent to load a class first, before trying itself.
This prevents core classes from being accidentally overridden by application code.

topics.heading: Interpreter vs JIT Compiler
topics.imageAlt: Diagram showing the Interpreter and JIT Compiler as two separate, equal components inside the JVM's Execution Engine, not one nested inside the other, with the Interpreter running bytecode immediately and profiling calls, and the JIT Compiler compiling hot methods to cached native code that later calls run directly
points: Bytecode is not machine code — the CPU can't run it directly.
The **Execution Engine** turns bytecode into machine code, in one of two ways.
**Interpreter** and **JIT Compiler** are two separate, equal components — siblings, not parent and child.
**Interpreter** — translates and runs one instruction at a time, every single time. Nothing is cached.
**JIT Compiler** — compiles an entire hot method to native code once, caches it, and reuses it after that.
**HotSpot** uses both: interprets first, then promotes a method to JIT-compiled code once it's proven hot.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["MyApp.java"] --> B["javac"]
    B --> C["MyApp.class - bytecode"]
    C --> D["ClassLoader loads the class"]
    D --> E["Execution Engine runs it"]
    E --> F["Native machine code on the CPU"]
architectureFlow.caption: From source file to running machine code — the full pipeline.

simpleExample.scenario: Comparing which ClassLoader loads a JDK class versus your own class.
simpleExample.input: Running this class from a normal classpath, with no custom class loaders configured.
simpleExample.execution: `String` is loaded by the Bootstrap ClassLoader; `ClassLoaderDemo` is loaded by the Application ClassLoader.
simpleExample.output: The first line prints `null`. The second prints something like `jdk.internal.loader.ClassLoaders$AppClassLoader@...`.
simpleExample.explanation: Core JDK classes and your own application classes are loaded by two different ClassLoaders in the same delegation chain.

realWorldExample.scenario: A nightly batch job recalculating settlement fees for millions of transactions in a tight loop.
realWorldExample.input: 20 million iterations calling `calculateFee` in a tight loop.
realWorldExample.execution: The first several thousand calls are interpreted. Once HotSpot detects `calculateFee` is hot, the JIT compiler compiles it to native code, and every remaining call uses that cached version.
realWorldExample.output: The batch job runs far faster per-iteration in its later stages than in its first few thousand iterations.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is why short-lived scripts don't benefit much from the JIT, but long-running batch jobs and servers do — there's enough repetition for HotSpot to notice and optimize.

comparisonTables.title: Interpreter vs JIT Compiler
rows: Code that runs only a few times
Only after a method is proven hot

bestPractices: Only install a full JDK on machines that build or compile code; end-user machines just need a runtime.
Don't assume a fresh JVM runs at full speed immediately — expect a brief JIT warm-up period, especially in microbenchmarks.
Print `SomeClass.class.getClassLoader()` when debugging classpath or "class not found" issues.
commonMistakes: Confusing JRE and JDK — assuming you can compile code with just a JRE installed.
Assuming the JIT compiles every method immediately at startup — it only compiles methods proven to be hot.
Treating a `null` result from `getClassLoader()` as an error, instead of recognizing the Bootstrap ClassLoader.
interviewTips: Draw the nesting relationship if asked: JDK contains JRE, JRE contains JVM.
If asked about JIT, say it's a sibling of the interpreter inside the Execution Engine, not a sub-component of it.

faq.q: Do I need a JDK to just run a Java application?
faq.a: No — a JRE (or a JDK, which includes one) is enough to run a compiled .class or .jar file. The JDK's compiler is only needed to build Java source code.

faq.q: Why does String.class.getClassLoader() return null?
faq.a: String is loaded by the Bootstrap ClassLoader, which is implemented in native code and has no corresponding Java object to return — Java represents that as null.

faq.q: Does every method eventually get JIT-compiled?
faq.a: No. Only methods that cross HotSpot's invocation threshold get compiled. Rarely-called methods stay interpreted for the program's whole life, since compiling them wouldn't be worth the cost.

summary: JVM executes bytecode. JRE adds the libraries needed to run a program. JDK adds the tools needed to build one.
A class goes through Loading, Linking, and Initialization, handled by a chain of ClassLoaders.
The Execution Engine runs bytecode via an Interpreter and a JIT Compiler working together, not one inside the other.
quickRevision: JVM = executes bytecode. JRE = JVM + libraries. JDK = JRE + dev tools.
Class loading has 3 phases: Loading, Linking, Initialization.
3 ClassLoaders, in delegation order: Bootstrap, Platform/Extension, Application.
The Bootstrap ClassLoader has no Java representation — `getClassLoader()` on a core class returns `null`.
The Execution Engine converts bytecode to machine code via the Interpreter or the JIT Compiler.
Interpreter and JIT are siblings inside the Execution Engine, not parent and child.
The JIT compiles a hot method to native code once, then reuses that cached version on every later call.
HotSpot uses tiered compilation: C1 (fast, light) then C2 (slower, heavily optimized).

beginner: What is the difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM?
What does a ClassLoader do?
What is the difference between the interpreter and the JIT compiler?
intermediate: What are the three phases of class loading?
Why does the Bootstrap ClassLoader return null from getClassLoader()?
Why do long-running programs get faster per-iteration over time?
advanced: Explain tiered compilation (C1 vs C2) and why HotSpot uses both.
How does the ClassLoader delegation model prevent a malicious class from replacing java.lang.String?
Walk through what happens between running `java MyApp` and your first line of code executing.

title: Memory Management & GC
overview: The JVM manages memory automatically, across two very different areas.
The Heap stores objects. The Stack stores method calls and local variables.
Garbage Collection is what reclaims Heap memory nobody is using anymore.
why: A program creates far more objects than it could ever track and free by hand.
Manual memory management is exactly what caused many classic C/C++ bugs.
Java needs an automatic system to reclaim memory without the programmer's help.
problem: In a language with manual memory management, every allocated object must be freed explicitly.
Forgetting to free memory causes a leak that slowly exhausts available RAM.
Freeing memory too early, while something still references it, causes a crash or corrupted data.
Getting this right by hand, across a large codebase, is extremely error-prone.

intro: Two memory areas, one generational strategy, and one collection process.

topics.heading: Heap vs Stack
topics.imageAlt: Diagram of the Java heap split into Young Generation (Eden, Survivor S0, Survivor S1) and Old Generation, alongside per-thread Stack and Metaspace
points: **Heap** — shared across all threads. Stores every object and array.
Managed by the Garbage Collector. `OutOfMemoryError` if it fills up.
**Stack** — one per thread. Stores method call frames and local variables.
LIFO order. `StackOverflowError` if it grows too deep.

topics.heading: Heap Generations
points: **Young Generation** — Eden plus two Survivor spaces (S0, S1).
Most objects die young, so this area is collected frequently and cheaply — a **Minor GC**.
**Promotion** — an object surviving enough Minor GC cycles gets moved to the **Old Generation**.
**Old Generation** — long-lived objects. Collected less often, but a **Major/Full GC** here is expensive.
**Metaspace** (Java 8+) — native memory storing class metadata, not part of the heap.

topics.heading: Garbage Collection
points: **GC Roots** — local variables, active threads, static fields. Anything reachable from a root is alive.
**Basic algorithm** — Mark (find reachable objects), Sweep (reclaim the rest), Compact (defragment).
**GC algorithms** — Serial, Parallel, **G1** (default since Java 9), **ZGC**/Shenandoah (very low pause times).
**`System.gc()`** is only a suggestion to the JVM — it doesn't force collection to run immediately.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["New object created"] --> B["Eden - Young Generation"]
    B --> C{"Survives Minor GC?"}
    C -->|No| E["Reclaimed"]
    C -->|Yes| D["Survivor Space"]
    D --> F{"Survives enough cycles?"}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G["Promoted to Old Generation"]
    G --> H{"Survives Major GC?"}
    H -->|No| E
    H -->|Yes| G
architectureFlow.caption: An object's journey from creation to either promotion or reclamation.

simpleExample.scenario: A recursive method with no base case, exhausting the stack.
simpleExample.input: No base case — `recurse()` always calls itself.
simpleExample.execution: Each call pushes a new frame onto the current thread's stack. Since `recurse()` never returns, frames pile up until the stack's fixed size is exhausted.
simpleExample.output: A `StackOverflowError` is thrown, and the number of calls made before the crash is printed.
simpleExample.explanation: Each thread's stack is finite and isn't managed by the Garbage Collector at all — filling it crashes that thread specifically.

realWorldExample.scenario: An OutOfMemoryError caused by an unbounded in-memory cache.
realWorldExample.input: `cacheMerchant()` is called for millions of distinct merchant IDs over the app's lifetime, with no eviction policy.
realWorldExample.execution: Every `Merchant` object stays reachable through the static `cache` map, so the Garbage Collector can never reclaim any of them, no matter how much GC runs.
realWorldExample.output: Eventually, `OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space` is thrown once the Old Generation is full and nothing can be reclaimed.
realWorldExample.explanation: GC only reclaims unreachable objects. A static, ever-growing map keeps every entry reachable forever — this looks like a GC problem but is really an application design bug.

comparisonTables.title: Minor GC vs Major/Full GC
rows: Expensive, can pause the app noticeably

bestPractices: Let objects go out of scope naturally instead of manually nulling references — the GC already handles this.
Avoid unbounded static collections that keep growing — they defeat garbage collection entirely.
Pick a GC algorithm based on actual latency requirements — G1 is a safe default; ZGC for very low pause times.
commonMistakes: Assuming `System.gc()` forces an immediate collection — it's only a suggestion to the JVM.
Storing objects forever in a static `Map` or `List`, causing a slow `OutOfMemoryError` instead of a fast crash.
Confusing `StackOverflowError` (recursion/stack issue) with `OutOfMemoryError` (heap issue) — different causes and fixes.
interviewTips: If asked why most objects die young, name the weak generational hypothesis directly.
Know the difference between Minor GC and Major/Full GC, and which generation each one targets.

faq.q: Does calling System.gc() free memory immediately?
faq.a: No. It only suggests to the JVM that now might be a good time to run garbage collection. The JVM is free to ignore that suggestion entirely.

faq.q: Why does Java have generations instead of one big heap?
faq.a: Most objects become garbage very quickly (the weak generational hypothesis). Splitting the heap lets the JVM scan the small, fast-changing Young Generation often and cheaply, instead of scanning the entire heap every time.

faq.q: Can the Stack run out of memory the same way the Heap does?
faq.a: Yes, but it throws a different, specific error: StackOverflowError, not OutOfMemoryError. It usually comes from deep or infinite recursion, not from allocating too many objects.

summary: The Heap stores objects and is managed by the Garbage Collector; the Stack stores method calls, managed automatically per thread.
Objects start in the Young Generation and get promoted to the Old Generation if they survive enough GC cycles.
Garbage Collection only reclaims unreachable objects — a live reference anywhere keeps an object alive indefinitely.
quickRevision: Heap stores objects, shared across all threads, managed by GC.
Stack stores method frames and locals, one per thread, LIFO.
Heap failure: `OutOfMemoryError`. Stack failure: `StackOverflowError`.
Young Generation = Eden + two Survivor spaces. Most objects die here.
An object surviving enough Minor GCs gets promoted to the Old Generation.
Minor GC collects the Young Generation; Major/Full GC collects the Old Generation and is far more expensive.
G1 is the default GC since Java 9; ZGC/Shenandoah target very low pause times.
`System.gc()` is only a suggestion — it does not force collection.

beginner: What is the difference between the Heap and the Stack?
What causes a StackOverflowError?
What is the default garbage collector in modern Java?
intermediate: What is the weak generational hypothesis?
What's the difference between Minor GC and Major/Full GC?
Why doesn't `System.gc()` guarantee an immediate collection?
advanced: Explain how an object gets promoted from the Young Generation to the Old Generation.
How would you diagnose a slow memory leak caused by a static cache that never evicts entries?
When would you choose ZGC over G1, and what's the tradeoff?

title: Keywords
overview: Java has reserved keywords that control behavior at the language level.
This page covers the ones used most often outside basic syntax like `if` or `for`.
A few are specifically about multithreading; the rest apply everywhere.
why: These keywords show up constantly in real code and in interviews.
Misusing one of them, especially the multithreading ones, causes subtle, hard-to-find bugs.
Knowing exactly what each one guarantees — and doesn't — separates surface knowledge from real understanding.
problem: Without keywords like `static` or `final`, every class member would behave identically.
There would be no way to declare a class-wide constant or a method that doesn't need an object.
Without `synchronized` and `volatile`, there would be no language-level way to coordinate shared data between threads.

intro: Split into general-purpose keywords, and the ones specific to multithreading.

topics.heading: General Purpose Keywords
points: **final** — variable: can't be reassigned. Method: can't be overridden. Class: can't be extended.
**static** — belongs to the class, not an instance. Shared across all objects.
**this** — refers to the current instance. Resolves naming ambiguity, chains constructors.
**super** — refers to the parent class. Calls the parent constructor or a parent method.
**abstract** — method: no body, implemented by a subclass. Class: can't be instantiated.
**transient** — marks a field to be skipped during serialization.
**instanceof** — a runtime type check. Java 16+ supports pattern matching directly in the check.

topics.heading: Multithreading-Related Keywords
points: **synchronized** — method or block level. Only one thread can hold the lock at a time.
**volatile** — guarantees visibility of a variable's latest value across threads.
It does **not** guarantee atomicity — `count++` on a `volatile int` is still a race condition.
**wait() / notify() / notifyAll()** — methods on `Object`, used for inter-thread communication.
They must be called from inside a `synchronized` block.

simpleExample.scenario: Demonstrating that a static field is shared across every instance, while an instance field is not.
simpleExample.input: Two `Counter` objects created one after another.
simpleExample.execution: Each constructor call increments the single shared `totalCount` and assigns the current value to that instance's own `instanceId`.
simpleExample.output: `1 2 2` — `a.instanceId` is 1, `b.instanceId` is 2, and `Counter.totalCount` is 2, shared by both.
simpleExample.explanation: `totalCount` belongs to the class itself, not to any one object — every instance sees and modifies the exact same field.

realWorldExample.scenario: A background thread processing payment retries until a stop flag is set from the main thread.
realWorldExample.input: The main thread calls `worker.shutdown()` while the worker thread is mid-loop.
realWorldExample.execution: Because `running` is `volatile`, the write from `shutdown()` is immediately visible to the worker thread's next read of `running`, without needing `synchronized`.
realWorldExample.output: The worker thread's `while (running)` loop exits on its next check, and the thread finishes cleanly.
realWorldExample.explanation: Without `volatile`, the worker thread could keep reading a cached copy of `running` from before the shutdown, and loop forever even though the main thread already set it to `false`.

comparisonTables.title: synchronized vs volatile
rows: A simple flag read/written by multiple threads

bestPractices: Use `volatile` only for simple flags or references, never for compound operations like incrementing a counter.
Prefer `final` for anything that shouldn't change after construction — it documents intent and catches mistakes at compile time.
Reach for `synchronized` or `java.util.concurrent` classes when multiple threads need to coordinate on more than a single flag.
commonMistakes: Using `volatile` on a counter and assuming `count++` is now thread-safe — it isn't, since increment is read-modify-write.
Calling `wait()`/`notify()` outside a `synchronized` block, which throws `IllegalMonitorStateException`.
Marking a class `final` and later being surprised it can't be extended for a legitimate new use case.
interviewTips: If asked to compare `synchronized` and `volatile`, lead with: `volatile` only guarantees visibility, `synchronized` guarantees visibility and atomicity together.
Be ready with the `this()` vs `super()` constructor-chaining example — a common quick-fire interview question.

faq.q: Does volatile make count++ thread-safe?
faq.a: No. count++ is actually three separate steps — read, increment, write — and volatile only guarantees each individual read or write is visible across threads, not that the whole sequence is atomic. Use AtomicInteger or synchronized instead.

faq.q: Can wait() be called outside a synchronized block?
faq.a: No — it throws IllegalMonitorStateException. wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() all require the calling thread to already hold the object's monitor lock, which only synchronized provides.

faq.q: What's the difference between final and static final?
faq.a: final alone means the field can't be reassigned after being set once per instance. static final means the field belongs to the class (one shared copy) and can't be reassigned — the usual way to declare a true constant.

summary: `final`, `static`, `this`, `super`, `abstract`, `transient`, and `instanceof` each control a different aspect of class behavior.
`synchronized` and `volatile` are Java's core language-level tools for thread safety, but they solve different problems.
`volatile` guarantees visibility only; `synchronized` guarantees both visibility and atomicity, at the cost of blocking other threads.
quickRevision: `final`: no reassignment (variable), no override (method), no subclassing (class).
`static`: belongs to the class, shared across every instance.
`this` refers to the current instance; `super` refers to the parent class.
`abstract` method: no body, must be implemented by a subclass.
`transient`: skipped during serialization.
`instanceof`: runtime type check, supports pattern matching since Java 16.
`synchronized`: only one thread at a time, guarantees visibility AND atomicity.
`volatile`: guarantees visibility only, not atomicity — never enough alone for `count++`.

beginner: What does the `final` keyword do on a variable, a method, and a class?
What is the difference between `this` and `super`?
What does the `transient` keyword do?
intermediate: What is the difference between `synchronized` and `volatile`?
Why doesn't `volatile` make `count++` thread-safe?
When would you use `static` instead of an instance field?
advanced: Why must `wait()`/`notify()` be called from inside a `synchronized` block?
How does the JVM implement the visibility guarantee that `volatile` provides?
Give an example where relying only on `volatile` for a shared counter would cause a real production bug.

title: OOPs Concepts
overview: Object-Oriented Programming organizes code around objects instead of a sequence of instructions.
Four principles make this style work: encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction.
Association, aggregation, and composition describe how objects relate to each other.
why: Real systems model real-world entities: a User, an Order, a Payment, a Merchant.
OOP gives a structured way to represent those entities and their relationships in code.
Almost every Java interview tests these principles directly, with a live example.
problem: Without structure, a large codebase becomes one long procedure acting on loose data.
Changing one piece of data's shape means finding and fixing every place that touches it.
There's no way to hide internal details, reuse behavior across similar entities, or treat different entities uniformly.

intro: Four pillars, plus three ways objects relate to each other.

topics.heading: Encapsulation
points: Bundling data and behavior into one class, controlling access via `private`/`protected`/`public`.
Example: an `Order` class keeps `total` `private`, only changeable through `addItem()`.
No outside code can set an invalid total directly.

topics.heading: Inheritance
points: A class acquires fields and methods from another using `extends`.
Java supports single inheritance for classes, but multiple inheritance of behavior via interfaces.
Example: `class UpiPayment extends Payment` reuses `Payment`'s `validate()`/`refund()` and adds its own `verifyVpa()`.

topics.heading: Polymorphism
points: **Compile-time (overloading)** — same method name, different parameter list, resolved at compile time.
Example: `calculateTotal(Cart cart)` vs `calculateTotal(Cart cart, Coupon coupon)`.
**Runtime (overriding)** — a subclass provides its own version of a parent method, resolved at runtime.
Example: `PaymentGateway gateway = new RazorpayGateway(); gateway.charge(amount);` runs `RazorpayGateway`'s own `charge()`.

topics.heading: Abstraction
points: Hiding *how* something works and exposing only *what* it does, via interfaces or abstract classes.
Example: `PaymentGateway gateway = new RazorpayGateway();` — calling code only knows the `PaymentGateway` contract.
It doesn't need to know how `RazorpayGateway` actually talks to Razorpay's API internally.

topics.heading: Association, Aggregation & Composition
topics.imageAlt: Diagram contrasting association (a plain link between Teacher and Student), aggregation (a hollow-diamond has-a link between Department and Employee), and composition (a filled-diamond has-a link between House and Room)
points: **Association** — a general "uses-a" relationship between two independent classes.
Example: a `Customer` and a `Merchant` they've never ordered from before.
**Aggregation** — a "has-a" relationship where the parts can exist independently of the whole.
Example: a `Cart` has `Product`s, but a `Product` can exist without that particular `Cart`.
**Composition** — a stronger "has-a" where the part's lifecycle is tied to the whole.
Example: an `Order` has `OrderLineItem`s — delete the `Order`, and its line items go with it.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["PaymentGateway gateway = new RazorpayGateway()"] --> B["gateway.charge(amount) called"]
    B --> C["JVM checks the actual runtime type"]
    C --> D["RazorpayGateway.charge() runs"]
architectureFlow.caption: Runtime polymorphism: the declared type is PaymentGateway, but the actual object decides which code runs.

simpleExample.scenario: Preventing an Order's total from being set to an invalid value directly.
simpleExample.execution: `addItem()` checks the price before touching `total`, and rejects the negative value immediately.
simpleExample.output: An `IllegalArgumentException` is thrown; `total` is never modified.
simpleExample.explanation: Because `total` is `private`, the only way to change it is through `addItem()`, which enforces the validation rule every time — there's no way to bypass it.

realWorldExample.scenario: Charging a customer through whichever payment gateway is configured for a merchant, without the calling code knowing which one it is.
realWorldExample.input: `processPayment(new RazorpayGateway(), amount)` for one merchant, `processPayment(new PhonePeGateway(), amount)` for another.
realWorldExample.execution: `gateway.charge(amount)` always calls the actual object's own `charge()` method, resolved at runtime based on the object's real class.
realWorldExample.output: The Razorpay call runs Razorpay's `charge()`; the PhonePe call runs PhonePe's — through the exact same `processPayment()` method.
realWorldExample.explanation: `processPayment()` never needs an `if`/`else` checking which gateway it got — polymorphism handles the branching automatically, based on the object's real type.

bestPractices: Keep fields private by default; expose behavior through methods, not direct field access.
Favor composition over inheritance when a class merely needs another class's behavior, not its full identity.
Always add `@Override` when overriding a method — it catches signature typos at compile time.
commonMistakes: Making every field public "to keep things simple" — this defeats encapsulation entirely.
Confusing overloading and overriding — overloading is same-class/compile-time, overriding is parent-child/runtime.
Using inheritance where composition would fit better, creating deep, fragile class hierarchies.
interviewTips: Have one crisp real example ready for each of the four pillars — interviewers almost always ask for one.
Be ready to explain aggregation vs composition with the "does the part outlive the whole" test.

faq.q: Can a class have both overloaded and overridden versions of the same method name?
faq.a: Yes. A subclass can override one specific overload of a parent method while also adding entirely new overloads of its own — they're independent mechanisms.

faq.q: Is composition always better than inheritance?
faq.a: Not always, but it's usually the safer default. Inheritance still fits well when a subclass genuinely IS-A more specific version of its parent, like `UpiPayment` IS-A `Payment`.

faq.q: What's the real difference between abstraction and encapsulation?
faq.a: Encapsulation hides an object's internal data behind methods. Abstraction hides implementation complexity behind a simpler interface. They're related but distinct — encapsulation is about data, abstraction is about complexity.

summary: Encapsulation hides data behind controlled access. Inheritance reuses behavior across related classes.
Polymorphism lets one method call behave differently based on the actual object's type — at compile time (overloading) or runtime (overriding).
Abstraction hides implementation complexity behind a simple interface; association, aggregation, and composition describe how objects depend on each other.
quickRevision: Encapsulation: bundle data + behavior, control access with private/protected/public.
Inheritance: reuse fields/methods via `extends`; single inheritance for classes, multiple via interfaces.
Polymorphism: overloading (compile-time, same class) vs overriding (runtime, parent-child).
Abstraction: hide how, expose what, via interfaces or abstract classes.
Association: a general uses-a relationship between independent classes.
Aggregation: has-a, weak ownership — the part can exist without the whole.
Composition: has-a, strong ownership — the part's lifecycle is tied to the whole.
`@Override` on an overriding method catches signature mistakes at compile time.

beginner: What are the four pillars of OOP?
What is the difference between a class and an object?
What does encapsulation mean in practice?
intermediate: What's the difference between method overloading and overriding?
What's the difference between aggregation and composition?
Why would you use an interface instead of an abstract class?
advanced: How does the JVM resolve which overridden method to call at runtime?
When would deep inheritance hierarchies become a liability, and how would composition fix it?
Design a small class hierarchy for different payment methods (UPI, Card, Wallet) using these principles.

title: Wrapper Classes
overview: Every Java primitive type has a matching object wrapper class.
Wrappers let primitives work anywhere Java requires an object, like in collections.
They also introduce a few sharp edges around equality and caching.
why: Java's generics system only works with objects, never with primitives directly.
A `List<int>` won't compile, but a `List<Integer>` will.
Wrapper classes bridge this gap between primitives and the object-based parts of the language.
problem: Collections like `List` and `Map` are built entirely around objects.
A primitive `int` has no methods, no null state, and can't be a generic type parameter.
Without wrapper classes, primitives couldn't be stored in a `List`, returned as an `Object`, or represent a missing value.

intro: Wrappers, and the automatic conversion between them and primitives.

topics.heading: What & Why
points: Every primitive type has a corresponding wrapper class: `int`→`Integer`, `double`→`Double`, `boolean`→`Boolean`, `char`→`Character`.
Needed because generics and collections only accept objects, never primitives.

topics.heading: Autoboxing & Unboxing
points: **Autoboxing** — the compiler automatically converts a primitive to a wrapper.
`Integer i = 5;` really calls `Integer.valueOf(5)` behind the scenes.
**Unboxing** — the compiler automatically converts a wrapper back to a primitive.
`int x = i;` really calls `i.intValue()` behind the scenes.

topics.heading: Common Pitfalls
points: **Integer cache** — `Integer` caches values from **-128 to 127**.
Two cached `Integer`s with the same value can be `==` equal.
Two `Integer`s outside that range are always different objects, even with the same value.
Always use `.equals()` to compare wrapper values, never `==`.
**NullPointerException risk** — unboxing a `null` wrapper throws an NPE.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Integer a = 100"] --> B{"Value between -128 and 127?"}
    B -->|Yes| C["Return the cached Integer object"]
    B -->|No| D["Create a new Integer object"]
architectureFlow.caption: Why == sometimes works on Integers, and sometimes doesn't.

simpleExample.scenario: Storing primitive int values in a List, which only accepts objects.
simpleExample.input: Two `int` literals, `5` and `10`, added to a `List<Integer>`.
simpleExample.execution: The compiler converts each `int` to an `Integer` via autoboxing before `add()` runs, and converts back via unboxing when `get(0)` is assigned to `first`.
simpleExample.output: `first` is `5`, and `quantities` holds two `Integer` objects internally.
simpleExample.explanation: `List<Integer>` only works because `Integer` is an object — `List<int>` wouldn't compile. Autoboxing and unboxing make this conversion invisible in the source code.

realWorldExample.scenario: Comparing two order quantities with == instead of equals(), and getting inconsistent results.
realWorldExample.input: Two pairs of `Integer` values: 100/100 (inside the cache range) and 200/200 (outside it).
realWorldExample.execution: 100 falls within the Integer cache's -128 to 127 range, so `quantityA` and `quantityB` point to the same cached object. 200 falls outside that range, so `largeQtyA` and `largeQtyB` are separate objects.
realWorldExample.output: The first `println` prints `true`. The second prints `false` — even though both pairs represent equal values.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is exactly why `Integer` values must be compared with `.equals()`, never `==`. The second comparison fails purely because of an internal caching detail that has nothing to do with the actual values.

comparisonTables.title: Primitive vs Wrapper
rows: Larger, an object on the heap

bestPractices: Always use `.equals()` to compare wrapper values, never `==`.
Prefer primitives over wrappers when `null` is never a valid state, for both performance and safety.
Use wrapper types deliberately when a field genuinely needs to represent "no value".
commonMistakes: Comparing two `Integer` objects with `==` and assuming it always works because it happened to work for small values.
Unboxing a `null` wrapper without a null check, causing an unexpected `NullPointerException`.
Using wrapper types everywhere by default, even when a field can never legitimately be null.
interviewTips: The Integer cache question (-128 to 127) is one of the most common Java trick questions — know the exact range.
If asked why `==` can behave inconsistently on Integers, explain the caching mechanism, not just "use equals instead".

faq.q: Why does the Integer cache only go from -128 to 127?
faq.a: Small integer values are extremely common in real programs, so the JVM pre-creates and caches Integer objects in that range to avoid constantly allocating new ones. It's a memory/performance optimization, with that range guaranteed by the JLS.

faq.q: Does the Integer cache apply to other wrapper types?
faq.a: Yes — Byte, Short, Long, and Character have similar caching for small values, and Boolean caches both TRUE and FALSE, since there are only two possible values.

faq.q: Why does unboxing a null Integer throw an NPE?
faq.a: Unboxing calls a method like intValue() on the wrapper object to get the primitive value. If the reference is null, there's no object to call that method on, so it throws NullPointerException like any other null method call.

summary: Wrapper classes let primitives work as objects, which generics and collections require.
Autoboxing and unboxing make the conversion between primitive and wrapper mostly invisible in code.
The Integer cache (-128 to 127) means `==` sometimes works and sometimes doesn't on equal-looking Integer values — always use `.equals()` instead.
quickRevision: Every primitive has a wrapper class: `int`→`Integer`, `double`→`Double`, `boolean`→`Boolean`, `char`→`Character`.
Wrappers exist because generics and collections only work with objects, not primitives.
Autoboxing: primitive → wrapper, automatic. Unboxing: wrapper → primitive, automatic.
Integer caches values from -128 to 127 — cached Integers can be `==` equal; others can't.
Never use `==` to compare wrapper objects — always use `.equals()`.
Unboxing a `null` wrapper throws `NullPointerException`.
Other wrapper types (Byte, Short, Long, Character, Boolean) have similar caching behavior.

beginner: What is a wrapper class?
Give an example of a primitive type and its wrapper class.
What is autoboxing?
intermediate: Why does `Integer a = 100; Integer b = 100; a == b` return true, but the same code with 200 returns false?
Why can't you use a primitive type as a generic type parameter?
What happens if you unbox a null Integer?
advanced: What is the exact range of the Integer cache, and why was that range chosen?
Which other wrapper types besides Integer have caching, and how does Boolean's caching differ?
How would you safely compare two Integer values without risking the `==` caching pitfall?

title: String & String Pool
overview: `String` is one of the most-used types in Java, and one of the most misunderstood.
It's immutable — once created, its content never changes.
The String Pool is a memory optimization that only works because of that immutability.
why: Strings represent text everywhere — names, IDs, messages, URLs, JSON payloads.
A program creates enormous numbers of string literals, often repeating the same ones.
Java needs a way to avoid creating a new object for every repeated literal.
problem: If every `"hello"` in a program created a brand-new object, memory usage would balloon quickly.
If `String` were mutable, sharing one string between two places would be dangerous.
Changing it in one place would silently change it everywhere else it was referenced.

intro: Immutability enables the pool. The pool avoids duplicate objects for identical text.

topics.heading: Immutability & the String Pool
points: `String` is immutable — any "modification" (`concat`, `+`, `substring`) creates a new `String` object.
**String Constant Pool** — a memory area that stores string literals for reuse.
`String a = "hi"; String b = "hi";` — both point to the same pooled object, so `a == b` is `true`.
`String c = new String("hi");` forces a new object on the heap, outside the pool.
`a == c` is `false`, even though `a.equals(c)` is `true`.
`.intern()` manually looks up (or inserts) a string in the pool.

topics.heading: String vs StringBuilder vs StringBuffer
points: **String** — immutable. Best for fixed or rarely-changed text.
**StringBuilder** — mutable, not thread-safe, fast. Best for building strings in a single thread.
**StringBuffer** — mutable, thread-safe, slower than `StringBuilder`.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["String a equals hi"] --> B{"Is hi already in the pool?"}
    B -->|Yes| C["Reuse the existing pooled object"]
    B -->|No| D["Add hi to the pool as a new object"]
    E["new String of hi"] --> F["Always creates a new object on the heap"]
architectureFlow.caption: Literals go through the pool. new String(...) always bypasses it.

simpleExample.scenario: Comparing two string literals versus one created with new.
simpleExample.input: Two literal `"hi"` strings and one `new String("hi")`.
simpleExample.execution: `a` and `b` both resolve to the same pooled object at compile time. `c` explicitly allocates a new object on the heap, outside the pool.
simpleExample.explanation: `==` compares object identity. `a` and `b` share one object; `c` is a different object with the same content, which is why `.equals()` — comparing content — still returns `true`.

realWorldExample.scenario: Building a large CSV export string in a loop, the slow way versus the fast way.
realWorldExample.input: A list of 10,000 `Order` objects to export as CSV rows.
realWorldExample.execution: The `+=` version creates a brand-new `String` object on every single iteration, throwing the previous one away. The `StringBuilder` version mutates one internal buffer the whole time.
realWorldExample.output: Both produce the same CSV text, but the `+=` version allocates roughly 10,000 throwaway `String` objects; the `StringBuilder` version allocates close to none.
realWorldExample.explanation: For any loop building up text, `StringBuilder` avoids the garbage-collection pressure that repeated `String` concatenation creates.

bestPractices: Use `StringBuilder` inside any loop that builds up a string incrementally.
Always compare string content with `.equals()`, never `==`.
Reserve `StringBuffer` for the rare case where multiple threads genuinely build the same buffer together.
commonMistakes: Using `==` to compare strings, and having it "work" during testing only because both were literals.
Concatenating strings with `+=` inside a large loop instead of using `StringBuilder`.
Assuming `new String("x")` and `"x"` are the same object just because their content is identical.
interviewTips: If asked why String is immutable, mention the String Pool, thread-safety, and safe use as a `HashMap` key — all three depend on it.
Be ready to trace through an `==` vs `.equals()` example on a whiteboard, step by step.

faq.q: Why is String immutable in Java?
faq.a: Immutability makes the String Pool safe (a shared literal can't be changed by one piece of code and silently affect another), makes String safe to use as a HashMap key, and makes it safe to share between threads without synchronization.

faq.q: Does .intern() always help performance?
faq.a: Not always. It helps when many duplicate strings would otherwise exist, but calling it unnecessarily adds overhead of its own — it's a targeted optimization, not a default habit.

faq.q: Is StringBuilder ever the wrong choice over StringBuffer?
faq.a: Only when multiple threads mutate the exact same buffer concurrently — in that specific case, StringBuffer's synchronization is genuinely needed. Otherwise StringBuilder is faster and sufficient.

summary: String is immutable — every apparent modification actually creates a new object.
The String Pool reuses identical literals, which is only safe because String can't change after creation.
StringBuilder is the right tool for building up text in a loop; StringBuffer only matters when threads share the same buffer.
quickRevision: String is immutable — concat/+ /substring all create new objects.
The String Pool stores literals for reuse; part of the heap since Java 7.
Two identical literals share one pooled object; `new String(...)` always creates a separate one.
`==` compares identity; `.equals()` compares content — always use `.equals()` for strings.
`.intern()` manually pools a string.
StringBuilder: mutable, fast, not thread-safe.
StringBuffer: mutable, thread-safe, slower.
Concatenating in a loop with `+=` creates one throwaway String per iteration — use StringBuilder instead.

beginner: Why is String immutable in Java?
What is the difference between `==` and `.equals()` for strings?
What is the String Pool?
intermediate: What is the difference between StringBuilder and StringBuffer?
Why does `new String("hi") == "hi"` return false?
Why is concatenating strings in a loop with `+=` inefficient?
advanced: What does `.intern()` do internally, and when would you actually use it?
How does String's immutability make it safe to use as a HashMap key?
Where does the String Pool live in memory, and how has that changed across Java versions?

title: Exception Handling
overview: Exceptions are Java's mechanism for signaling that something went wrong.
Every exception is either checked (the compiler forces you to deal with it) or unchecked (it doesn't).
Handling them well means choosing the right place and strategy for each kind of failure.
why: Things fail — a network call times out, a file doesn't exist, input is invalid.
A program needs a structured way to signal failure and let calling code react to it.
Without exceptions, every method would need its own ad-hoc error-signaling convention.
problem: Returning a special error code (like `-1` or `null`) from a method is easy to forget to check.
An ignored error code lets the failure surface somewhere completely unrelated, much later.
There's no structured way to attach details about the failure, or force calling code to acknowledge it.

intro: The hierarchy, the syntax for handling, and where handling logic should actually live.

topics.heading: Exception Hierarchy
topics.imageAlt: Diagram of Throwable splitting into Error and Exception, where Error leads to OutOfMemoryError and StackOverflowError, and Exception splits into checked exceptions like IOException and the unchecked RuntimeException branch like NullPointerException
points: **Throwable** is the root, splitting into **Error** and **Exception**.
**Error** — serious JVM-level problems like `OutOfMemoryError`. Don't try to catch these.
**Checked Exceptions** — must be handled or declared at compile time, e.g. `IOException`.
**Unchecked Exceptions (`RuntimeException`)** — not enforced by the compiler, e.g. `NullPointerException`.

topics.heading: Try-Catch-Finally
points: Catch the most specific exception type first.
**Multi-catch (Java 7+)** — `catch (IOException | SQLException e)` handles multiple types in one block.
**finally** — always runs, even with a `return` in `try`. Avoid `return`/`throw` inside it.
**try-with-resources (Java 7+)** — automatically closes any `AutoCloseable` resource, even on exception.

topics.heading: Manual vs Global Exception Handling
points: **Manual handling** — a `try-catch` at the specific call site, deciding how to react right there.
Good for one-off, context-specific recovery, like retrying a single network call.
**Global handling** — one centralized place catches exceptions across the whole app.
In Spring, that's `@ControllerAdvice` + `@ExceptionHandler`.

topics.heading: Custom Exceptions & Best Practices
points: **Custom checked** — extends `Exception`, for recoverable business-rule violations.
**Custom unchecked** — extends `RuntimeException`, for programming errors.
Never catch a bare `Exception`. Always log before re-throwing.
**Exception chaining** — passing the original exception as a cause preserves it, retrievable via `getCause()`.

architectureFlow.caption: An uncaught exception keeps propagating up the call stack until something catches it, or nothing does.

simpleExample.scenario: Reading a file safely, making sure the resource always closes.
simpleExample.input: A file path that doesn't exist: `orders.csv` is missing.
simpleExample.execution: Opening the `FileReader` throws a `FileNotFoundException` (a subtype of `IOException`). The `catch` block matches it before it can propagate further.
simpleExample.output: `Could not read file: orders.csv (No such file or directory)` is printed; the program keeps running.
simpleExample.explanation: try-with-resources guarantees `reader.close()` runs automatically, even though the exception happened before any line was read — no manual `finally` block was needed.

realWorldExample.scenario: Rejecting a payment that exceeds a merchant's configured limit, with one consistent error response across the whole API.
realWorldExample.input: `charge(merchant, amount)` is called with an amount above `merchant.getLimit()`.
realWorldExample.execution: `charge()` throws `PaymentLimitExceededException`. Being unchecked, it propagates straight up through every calling method without needing to be declared, until `@ControllerAdvice` catches it.
realWorldExample.output: The client receives a `422` response with the exact limit-exceeded message, instead of a raw, unhandled `500` error.
realWorldExample.explanation: The exception carries the failure information from deep inside `PaymentService` all the way to the API boundary, with no intermediate method needing to know or care about it.

bestPractices: Catch the most specific exception type available, not a broad `Exception` or `Throwable`.
Always use try-with-resources for anything that implements `AutoCloseable`.
Use unchecked exceptions for programming errors, checked ones only when the caller can realistically recover.
commonMistakes: Catching `Exception` broadly and silently swallowing it, hiding real bugs.
Putting a `return` or `throw` inside a `finally` block, which silently discards the original exception.
Using exceptions for normal control flow instead of actual error conditions.
interviewTips: Draw the `Throwable` → `Error`/`Exception` → `RuntimeException` hierarchy from memory if asked.
Be ready to explain exactly why global exception handling is preferred over repeating try-catch in every controller.

faq.q: Should a custom exception be checked or unchecked?
faq.a: Make it checked only if you want to force the caller to explicitly handle or declare it — typically for recoverable business conditions. Make it unchecked for programming errors or conditions that would be tedious to declare everywhere, like most REST API validation failures.

faq.q: Does finally always run, even if try has a return statement?
faq.a: Yes. finally runs even when try (or catch) returns, breaks, or continues — the only case it might not run is if the JVM itself exits, e.g. via System.exit().

faq.q: Why is exception chaining important?
faq.a: Without it, catching a low-level exception and throwing a new, higher-level one loses the original cause entirely, making the real root of a production issue impossible to trace from the stack trace alone.

summary: Throwable splits into Error (don't catch) and Exception (checked or unchecked).
try-with-resources handles cleanup automatically for anything AutoCloseable.
Global exception handling centralizes error responses; custom exceptions carry meaningful, specific failure information.
quickRevision: `Throwable` → `Error` (JVM-level, don't catch) and `Exception` (application-level).
Checked exceptions: must handle or declare, e.g. `IOException`.
Unchecked exceptions (`RuntimeException`): not enforced by the compiler, e.g. `NullPointerException`.
try-with-resources auto-closes any `AutoCloseable` resource.
`finally` always runs, even with a `return` in `try` — never put `return`/`throw` inside it.
Manual handling = try-catch at the call site. Global handling = one centralized handler.
Custom exceptions can extend `Exception` (checked) or `RuntimeException` (unchecked).
Exception chaining preserves the original root cause, retrievable via `getCause()`.

beginner: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?
What does the finally block guarantee?
Name two examples of checked exceptions and two unchecked ones.
intermediate: Why is try-with-resources preferred over a manual finally block for closing resources?
What's the advantage of @ControllerAdvice over try-catch in every controller?
Should a custom exception extend Exception or RuntimeException?
advanced: Explain exception chaining and why getCause() matters in production debugging.
What happens if an exception is thrown inside a finally block that's already handling another exception?
Design a custom exception hierarchy for a payment system with at least 3 distinct failure types.

title: Collection Framework
overview: The Collections Framework is Java's standard toolkit for storing and organizing groups of objects.
It's built as a small set of interfaces, each with multiple concrete implementations to choose from.
This is one of the most heavily tested topics in any Java interview.
why: Almost every program stores multiple items: a list of orders, a set of unique customer IDs, a map of prices.
Writing a custom data structure for every one of these needs would be wasteful and error-prone.
The Collections Framework provides battle-tested, general-purpose implementations for all of them.
problem: Before a unified framework, Java had a handful of unrelated, inconsistent collection classes.
`Vector`, `Hashtable`, and plain arrays each had their own quirks and no shared contract.
Code written against one couldn't easily be swapped for another, and there was no consistent way to iterate over all of them.

intro: The framework breaks down into a hierarchy, concrete implementations, internal mechanics, and iteration behavior.

topics.heading: Collection Hierarchy
topics.imageAlt: Diagram showing Iterable at the top, Collection extending it, List/Set/Queue extending Collection, and Map as a separate hierarchy that does not extend Collection
points: **Iterable** is the root — anything implementing it can be used in a for-each loop.
**Collection** extends `Iterable`. **List**, **Set**, and **Queue** all extend `Collection`.
**Map** is a separate hierarchy — it stores key-value pairs, not single elements.

topics.heading: List / Set / Map / Queue Implementations
points: **ArrayList** — dynamic array, O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete in the middle.
**LinkedList** — doubly-linked list, O(1) insert/delete at the ends, O(n) random access.
**HashSet** — no duplicates, no order guarantee, O(1) add/remove/contains.
**TreeSet / TreeMap** — sorted order, O(log n) operations.
**HashMap** — key-value pairs, O(1) average get/put. Not thread-safe.
**LinkedHashMap** — like `HashMap` but preserves insertion order.
**PriorityQueue** — orders elements by priority, O(log n) insert, O(1) peek.
Always declare with the interface (`List`, `Map`), instantiate with the implementation.

topics.heading: ArrayList Internal Working
topics.imageAlt: Diagram of ArrayList's backing array filling up and resizing to 1.5x capacity via Arrays.copyOf when full
points: Backed by a plain `Object[]` array, default initial capacity **10**.
When full, `add()` allocates a new array at roughly **1.5x** capacity and copies everything over.
This makes `add()` amortized O(1), not always O(1).
`get(index)` is O(1). Insert/remove in the middle is O(n) — elements shift.

topics.heading: HashMap Internal Working
topics.imageAlt: Diagram showing a key's hashCode being spread and reduced to a bucket index, with a collision handled by a linked list of entries
points: Backed by an array of buckets, default capacity **16**, default load factor **0.75**.
A key's hash is spread, then reduced to a bucket index.
Collisions are stored as a linked list in that bucket.
**Treeification (Java 8+)** — a bucket with 8+ entries, in a table of 64+ buckets, becomes a red-black tree.
**Resize** — past the load-factor threshold, capacity doubles and every entry is rehashed.

topics.heading: Fail-Fast vs Fail-Safe Iterators
points: **Fail-fast** (`ArrayList`, `HashMap`) — tracks a `modCount`. A structural change during iteration throws `ConcurrentModificationException`.
**Fail-safe** (`CopyOnWriteArrayList`, `ConcurrentHashMap`) — iterates a snapshot or lock-free structure. Never throws, but may not see the very latest changes.

topics.heading: Concurrent Collections
points: **ConcurrentHashMap** — thread-safe without locking the whole map. No null keys or values.
**CopyOnWriteArrayList** — every write copies the backing array. Reads never block.
**BlockingQueue** — `put()` blocks if full, `take()` blocks if empty. The standard producer-consumer building block.

topics.heading: Iterable & Iterator
points: **Iterable** — a single `iterator()` method; what makes a for-each loop possible.
**Iterator** — `hasNext()`, `next()`, `remove()`. The only safe way to remove while looping.
**ListIterator** — adds backward traversal and in-place `add()`/`set()` for `List`s.

topics.heading: Important Collection Methods
points: **Common to all** — `add()`, `remove()`, `contains()`, `size()`, `retainAll()`, `removeAll()`, `stream()`.
**Map-specific** — `getOrDefault()`, `putIfAbsent()`, `computeIfAbsent()`, `merge()`, `entrySet()`.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Iterator created - modCount snapshot saved"] --> B["Loop calls iterator.next()"]
    B --> C{"Current modCount equals snapshot?"}
    C -->|Yes| D["Return the next element"]
    C -->|No| E["Throw ConcurrentModificationException"]
architectureFlow.caption: How a fail-fast iterator actually detects a concurrent structural change.

simpleExample.scenario: Storing and looking up product prices by product ID.
simpleExample.input: A lookup for `SKU-9999`, a product ID that was never added.
simpleExample.execution: `getOrDefault()` checks the internal buckets for a matching key, finds none, and immediately returns the supplied default instead of `null`.
simpleExample.explanation: `getOrDefault()` avoids a manual null-check-then-fallback pattern, and avoids a `NullPointerException` if the caller forgot to check for a missing key.

realWorldExample.scenario: Removing out-of-stock products from a shopping cart while iterating over it.
realWorldExample.input: A cart containing at least one out-of-stock product alongside in-stock ones.
realWorldExample.execution: The for-each version calls `list.remove()` directly, changing the list's `modCount` mid-iteration; the next `next()` call detects the mismatch and throws. The `Iterator` version calls `it.remove()`, which updates `modCount` consistently.
realWorldExample.output: The for-each version throws `ConcurrentModificationException`. The `Iterator` version removes every out-of-stock product and completes normally.
realWorldExample.explanation: Modifying a list directly while iterating it with a for-each loop is a textbook fail-fast trigger — the fix is always the `Iterator`'s own `remove()`.

comparisonTables.title: HashMap vs Hashtable
rows: One null key, multiple null values

comparisonTables.title: Fail-Fast vs Fail-Safe Iterators
rows: Iterates a copy or lock-free structure

bestPractices: Declare collection variables with the interface type (`List`, `Set`, `Map`), not the concrete class.
Use `ConcurrentHashMap` or `CopyOnWriteArrayList`, not manual synchronization, for collections shared across threads.
Always remove elements during iteration through the `Iterator` itself, never through the collection directly.
commonMistakes: Calling `list.remove()` directly inside a for-each loop over that same list.
Using a `HashMap` when insertion order matters — reach for `LinkedHashMap` instead.
Assuming `ArrayList` and `LinkedList` perform the same — `LinkedList`'s O(1) is only at the ends, not the middle.
interviewTips: The ArrayList and HashMap internal-working diagrams are extremely common whiteboard questions — practice drawing them from memory.
If asked about fail-fast vs fail-safe, mention the actual mechanism (`modCount` checks vs snapshot iteration), not just "one throws and one doesn't".

faq.q: Why does ArrayList have amortized O(1) add() instead of always O(1)?
faq.a: Most calls to add() are O(1) — just placing the element in the next free slot. Occasionally, when the backing array is full, add() has to allocate a bigger array and copy every element into it, an O(n) operation for that one call. Averaged over many calls, this works out to O(1) amortized.

faq.q: Why does HashMap treeify a bucket instead of just growing the linked list?
faq.a: A very long linked list in one bucket means O(n) worst-case lookup for every key that lands there. Converting that bucket to a red-black tree caps worst-case lookup at O(log n) instead.

faq.q: Can I safely remove from a Map while iterating its entrySet()?
faq.a: Not directly through the Map — that triggers the same fail-fast ConcurrentModificationException. Use entrySet().iterator() and call the iterator's own remove() method instead.

summary: The Collections Framework is built on a small set of core interfaces — `Collection`, `List`, `Set`, `Queue`, and the separate `Map` hierarchy.
`ArrayList` and `HashMap` are the two most important implementations to understand internally.
Fail-fast iterators protect against silent bugs from concurrent modification; concurrent collections trade that guarantee for safe multi-threaded access.
quickRevision: Iterable → Collection → List/Set/Queue. Map is a separate hierarchy.
ArrayList: dynamic array, O(1) get, amortized O(1) add, O(n) middle insert/remove.
HashMap: bucket array, default capacity 16, load factor 0.75, treeifies at 8+ entries (table ≥ 64).
Fail-fast (ArrayList, HashMap) throws `ConcurrentModificationException` on concurrent structural change.
Fail-safe (CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap) never throws, may not see the very latest data.
`ConcurrentHashMap`: thread-safe, no locking the whole map, no null keys/values.
Always remove during iteration via `Iterator.remove()`, never the collection's own `remove()`.
Declare with the interface, instantiate with the implementation.

beginner: What is the difference between List, Set, and Map?
What is the default capacity of a HashMap?
What does the Iterable interface enable?
intermediate: How does ArrayList grow when it runs out of capacity?
What causes a ConcurrentModificationException, and how do you avoid it?
What is the difference between HashMap and Hashtable?
advanced: Explain exactly how a HashMap resolves a key to a bucket, including hash spreading.
What is treeification in HashMap, and what problem does it solve?
Design a thread-safe LRU cache using classes from the Collections Framework.

title: Collections Utility
overview: `java.util.Collections` is a utility class of static helper methods.
It operates on the data structures the Collections Framework provides — sorting, searching, wrapping, and creating them.
It's easy to confuse with the `Collection` interface, but they solve completely different problems.
why: Sorting, searching, and safely wrapping a collection are common needs across almost every program.
Reimplementing sort or binary search correctly, every time, wastes effort and invites bugs.
One shared, well-tested utility class covers all of these operations consistently.
problem: Without a shared utility class, every team would write its own sort, shuffle, and synchronization helpers.
Making a collection genuinely read-only, or safely accessible from multiple threads, needs careful wrapper code.
Getting those wrapper details wrong silently reintroduces the exact bugs a shared, tested utility is meant to prevent.

intro: Seven groups of static methods cover almost every common need.

topics.heading: What Is java.util.Collections
points: **`Collection`** (interface) — the root of the framework. That's the data structure.
**`Collections`** (class) — static helper methods that operate on those data structures. That's the toolbox.
You never instantiate `Collections` — every method is called directly on the class.

topics.heading: Sorting & Searching
points: **`sort(list)`** — sorts in natural order.
**`sort(list, comparator)`** — custom order.
**`binarySearch(list, key)`** — O(log n), but the list must already be sorted the same way.

topics.heading: Reordering
points: **`reverse(list)`** — reverses element order in place.
**`shuffle(list)`** — randomly permutes elements.
**`rotate(list, distance)`** — shifts every element, wrapping around.
**`swap(list, i, j)`** — swaps two elements in place.

topics.heading: Min, Max, Frequency & Comparison
points: **`min(collection)` / `max(collection)`** — natural order, or a custom `Comparator`.
**`frequency(collection, element)`** — counts occurrences.
**`disjoint(c1, c2)`** — `true` if the two collections share no elements.

topics.heading: Immutable & Thread-Safe Wrappers
points: **`unmodifiableList/Set/Map(c)`** — a read-only view. Modifying it throws `UnsupportedOperationException`.
It's a view — if the underlying collection changes directly, the view reflects that.
**`synchronizedList/Set/Map(c)`** — every method becomes `synchronized`.
Iterating it still needs a manual `synchronized` block around the loop.
Prefer `List.copyOf(list)` (Java 10+) for a true immutable copy, not just a view.

topics.heading: Empty & Singleton Factories
points: **`emptyList()` / `emptySet()` / `emptyMap()`** — a shared, immutable empty instance.
**`singletonList(obj)` / `singleton(obj)`** — an immutable collection with exactly one element.
**`nCopies(n, obj)`** — an immutable list with the same object repeated `n` times.

topics.heading: Bulk Operations
points: **`addAll(collection, elements...)`** — adds several elements in one call.
**`fill(list, obj)`** — replaces every element with the same object.
**`copy(dest, src)`** — copies elements into `dest`, which must already be at least as large as `src`.
**`replaceAll(list, oldVal, newVal)`** — replaces every occurrence of one value with another.

simpleExample.scenario: Exposing a merchant's order list as read-only to external callers.
simpleExample.execution: The returned list is a read-only view. Any structural modification attempt is checked and rejected before it reaches the real underlying list.
simpleExample.output: `UnsupportedOperationException` is thrown; the real `orders` list is untouched.
simpleExample.explanation: `unmodifiableList` doesn't copy the data — it wraps it. Changes made to the original `orders` list are still visible through this view, but the view itself can't be used to make changes.

realWorldExample.scenario: Sorting a batch of pending orders so the highest-value ones are processed first.
realWorldExample.input: A list of pending orders with varying total amounts.
realWorldExample.execution: `Collections.sort()` reorders the list in place using the comparator, comparing by `getTotal()` and reversing it to go highest-first.
realWorldExample.output: `pendingOrders` is sorted from highest total to lowest, and `highestValue` holds the single highest-value order.
realWorldExample.explanation: The comparator overrides any natural ordering `Order` might have, letting the same `sort()` method express any business ordering needed.

comparisonTables.title: Collection vs Collections
rows: No — implemented by ArrayList, HashSet, etc.

comparisonTables.title: unmodifiableList vs List.copyOf
rows: Reflects later changes to the original?
Exposing a live collection as read-only

bestPractices: Use `List.copyOf()` instead of `unmodifiableList()` when the caller must never see future changes to the original.
Always synchronize manually around iteration of a `synchronizedList()`, not just around individual method calls.
Reach for `Collections.emptyList()`/`singletonList()` instead of `new ArrayList<>()` for constant, small collections.
commonMistakes: Assuming `unmodifiableList()` returns an independent copy — it's a view, and the original can still change underneath it.
Iterating a `synchronizedList()` without wrapping the loop itself in a `synchronized` block.
Forgetting that `Collections.copy(dest, src)` requires `dest` to already be at least as large as `src`.
interviewTips: If asked "Collection vs Collections": one is an interface (the data structure), the other a utility class (the toolbox) — different things that just sound alike.
Know the difference between an unmodifiable view and a true immutable copy — a common follow-up question.

faq.q: Does unmodifiableList() make a collection truly immutable?
faq.a: No — it only prevents modification through that specific returned reference. If code elsewhere still holds a reference to the original mutable list and changes it, those changes appear through the unmodifiable view too.

faq.q: Why does Collections.synchronizedList() still need manual synchronization for iteration?
faq.a: Each individual method call is synchronized internally, but a for-each loop makes multiple calls in sequence. Without wrapping the whole loop in a synchronized block, another thread could modify the list between those calls.

faq.q: What happens if Collections.copy(dest, src) is called with a dest smaller than src?
faq.a: It throws IndexOutOfBoundsException immediately, since copy() only overwrites existing elements in dest and never grows it.

summary: `Collections` is a utility class of static helpers that operate on the framework's data structures.
It's easy to confuse with the `Collection` interface by name, but they solve entirely different problems.
Its wrapper methods return views over the original data, not independent copies — a common source of subtle bugs.
quickRevision: `Collection` = interface (the data structure). `Collections` = utility class (the toolbox).
`binarySearch()` requires the list already sorted the same way it's searched.
`reverse()`, `shuffle()`, `rotate()`, `swap()` all reorder a list in place.
`min()`/`max()`/`frequency()`/`disjoint()` answer common questions about a collection's contents.
`unmodifiableXxx()` returns a read-only view, not a copy.
`synchronizedXxx()` makes individual calls thread-safe, but iteration still needs a manual `synchronized` block.
`List.copyOf()` (Java 10+) makes a true immutable copy, unlike `unmodifiableList()`.
`emptyList()`/`singletonList()`/`nCopies()` are cheap, immutable factory methods for small cases.

beginner: What is the difference between Collection and Collections?
What does Collections.sort() require of the elements in the list?
What does Collections.emptyList() return?
intermediate: What's the difference between Collections.unmodifiableList() and List.copyOf()?
Why does binarySearch() require a pre-sorted list?
Why does iterating a synchronizedList() still need an extra synchronized block?
advanced: How would you build a truly immutable snapshot of a collection that's still being modified elsewhere?
What exception does Collections.copy() throw if the destination list is too small, and why?
Design a thread-safe read-mostly cache using Collections utility methods, and explain the tradeoffs versus ConcurrentHashMap.

title: Java 8+ Features
overview: Java 8 introduced functional programming concepts into a language that had been purely object-oriented.
Lambdas, the Stream API, and `Optional` are the three most-used additions.
They make working with collections and null values dramatically more concise.
why: Processing a list — filtering, transforming, collecting results — used to require verbose loops and anonymous classes.
Representing "a value that might not exist" used to mean `null`, with the risk of a `NullPointerException`.
Java 8 addressed both problems directly, without abandoning the language's object-oriented foundation.
problem: Passing behavior as a parameter meant writing a full anonymous inner class for a single method.
Filtering or transforming a list meant an explicit loop, an intermediate list, and manual bookkeeping.
A method returning `null` to mean "nothing found" gave callers no compile-time signal to check for that case.

intro: Lambdas need a functional interface to target. Streams process data through them. Optional models absence explicitly.

topics.heading: Functional Interfaces & Lambdas
points: **Functional interface** — exactly one abstract method. Enables lambda expressions.
**Lambda syntax** — `(params) -> expression`. Replaces verbose anonymous inner classes.
**Common built-ins** — `Predicate<T>` (test), `Function<T,R>` (apply), `Consumer<T>` (accept), `Supplier<T>` (get).
**Method References** — `Class::method`, shorthand for a lambda that just calls one method.
**Effectively final** — a lambda can only capture local variables never reassigned after their initial value.

topics.heading: Stream API
points: **Creating streams** — `collection.stream()`, `Stream.of(...)`, `Arrays.stream(arr)`.
**Intermediate operations** (lazy) — `filter()`, `map()`, `flatMap()`, `sorted()`, `limit()`.
**Terminal operations** (trigger execution) — `collect()`, `forEach()`, `reduce()`, `count()`.
**Collectors** — `toList()`, `toMap()`, `groupingBy()`, `partitioningBy()`, `joining()`.
**Parallel streams** — split work across threads. Use for CPU-heavy work; avoid for I/O-bound work.

topics.heading: Optional
points: **Creating** — `Optional.of(value)` (non-null), `Optional.ofNullable(value)` (may be null), `Optional.empty()`.
**Consuming safely** — `ifPresent()`, `orElse()`, `orElseGet()`, `orElseThrow()`.
**Transforming** — `.map()`, `.flatMap()`, `.filter()` chain operations without manual null checks.
Use `Optional` as a return type — not as a field or a method parameter.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Source: collection.stream()"] --> B["Intermediate: filter()"]
    B --> C["Intermediate: map()"]
    C --> D["Intermediate: sorted()"]
    D --> E["Terminal: collect()"]
