    E --> F["Result produced - pipeline runs only now"]
architectureFlow.caption: Nothing in the pipeline actually runs until the terminal operation is called.

simpleExample.scenario: Filtering a list of orders to only large ones, using a Predicate.
simpleExample.input: A list of orders with varying totals.
simpleExample.execution: `filter()` calls `isLargeOrder.test(order)` for each element, keeping only the ones where it returns `true`.
simpleExample.output: `largeOrders` contains only the orders with a total above 1000.
simpleExample.explanation: `isLargeOrder` is a `Predicate<Order>` — a functional interface with one abstract method, `test()`. The lambda is just a concise way of implementing that one method.

realWorldExample.scenario: Finding the most recent successful payment for a customer, safely handling the case where none exists.
realWorldExample.code:
Optional<Payment> latestPayment = payments.stream()
    .filter(p -> p.getCustomerId().equals(customerId))
    .filter(Payment::isSuccessful)
    .max(Comparator.comparing(Payment::getTimestamp));

String status = latestPayment
    .map(Payment::getStatus)
    .orElse("NO_PAYMENT_FOUND");
realWorldExample.input: A `customerId` with zero successful payments in the `payments` list.
realWorldExample.execution: The stream filters down to zero matching elements, so `max()` returns `Optional.empty()`. `map()` on an empty `Optional` does nothing and stays empty. `orElse()` then supplies the fallback.
realWorldExample.output: `status` is `"NO_PAYMENT_FOUND"`, with no `NullPointerException` anywhere in the chain.
realWorldExample.explanation: Every step in this chain is null-safe by construction — there's never a raw `null` flowing through, only an `Optional` that might or might not contain something.

comparisonTables.title: Anonymous Class vs Lambda
rows: Any interface, any number of methods

comparisonTables.title: map() vs flatMap()
rows: A stream, which then gets flattened
Can be a different number of elements
One-to-many, e.g. splitting a String into words

bestPractices: Keep lambda bodies short — extract complex logic into a named method reference instead.
Use `Optional` only as a method return type, never as a field or a method parameter.
Reach for `parallelStream()` only for genuinely CPU-heavy work on large datasets, and measure first.
commonMistakes: Calling `Optional.get()` without checking `isPresent()` first, defeating the whole purpose of `Optional`.
Using `flatMap()` where `map()` would do, or vice versa, producing a mis-shaped result.
Assuming a `Stream` can be reused after a terminal operation — streams can only be consumed once.
interviewTips: If asked to explain lazy evaluation, mention explicitly that nothing runs until a terminal operation is called.
Be ready to write a short Stream pipeline live — filter, map, collect is the most common pattern asked for.

faq.q: Can a Stream be reused after collect() has been called?
faq.a: No. Once a terminal operation runs, that stream is considered consumed, and calling any further operation on it throws IllegalStateException. A new stream must be created from the source again.

faq.q: Why prefer Optional.orElseGet() over Optional.orElse() sometimes?
faq.a: orElse() always evaluates its argument eagerly, even when the Optional has a value. orElseGet() takes a Supplier and only calls it when the Optional is actually empty — better when computing the fallback is expensive.

faq.q: What makes an interface a valid target for a lambda?
faq.a: It must be a functional interface — exactly one abstract method. It can have any number of default or static methods; only the single abstract method matters for lambda compatibility.

summary: Lambdas and functional interfaces let behavior be passed around as a value, replacing verbose anonymous classes.
The Stream API processes collections declaratively — nothing runs until a terminal operation.
Optional makes the possible absence of a value explicit and type-checked, instead of relying on null.
quickRevision: A functional interface has exactly one abstract method; enables lambdas.
Common built-ins: Predicate (test), Function (apply), Consumer (accept), Supplier (get).
Method references (`Class::method`) are shorthand for a lambda that just calls one existing method.
Stream intermediate ops are lazy — they don't run until a terminal op is called.
Terminal ops trigger the whole pipeline to actually execute.
A Stream can only be consumed once — reusing it after a terminal op throws `IllegalStateException`.
`Optional.of()` requires non-null; `Optional.ofNullable()` allows null; `Optional.empty()` has no value.
Use `Optional` as a return type only — never as a field or parameter.

beginner: What is a functional interface?
What is a lambda expression?
What is the purpose of Optional?
intermediate: What is the difference between intermediate and terminal Stream operations?
Why are Stream operations described as lazy?
What is the difference between map() and flatMap()?
advanced: Why can't a Stream be reused after a terminal operation, and what happens if you try?
When would parallelStream() actually hurt performance instead of helping?
Design a Stream pipeline that groups a list of orders by status and sums the total per group.

title: Multithreading & Concurrency
overview: Multithreading lets a single program run multiple sequences of instructions at the same time.
Java has built-in language support for creating threads and coordinating between them.
Getting synchronization wrong causes some of the hardest bugs to reproduce in software engineering.
why: A single-threaded server can only do one thing at a time, wasting CPU cores sitting idle.
Some tasks — a batch job, a background cache refresh — shouldn't block the main flow of a program.
Java needs threads, plus a way to keep them from corrupting shared data when they run at the same time.
problem: Running everything on one thread means a slow operation blocks everything else behind it.
Multiple threads reading and writing the same data at once can corrupt it in ways that are hard to reproduce.
Without language-level tools for creating and coordinating threads safely, every team would invent its own fragile solution.

intro: Creating threads, understanding their lifecycle, synchronizing shared data, and communicating between them.

topics.heading: Thread Basics & Ways to Create a Thread
points: **Extend `Thread`** — override `run()`, call `.start()`. Uses up the class's only extension slot.
**Implement `Runnable`** — pass it to `new Thread(runnable)`. Preferred — the class can still extend something else.
**Implement `Callable<V>`** — like `Runnable` but returns a value; submitted to an `ExecutorService`.
**Never call `run()` directly** — that runs the code on the current thread. Always call `start()`.

topics.heading: Thread Lifecycle & Types
topics.imageAlt: State diagram showing a thread moving from NEW to RUNNABLE to RUNNING to TERMINATED, with RUNNING able to move into a Blocked, Waiting, or Timed Waiting state and back
points: **Lifecycle** — NEW → RUNNABLE → RUNNING → BLOCKED/WAITING/TIMED_WAITING → TERMINATED.
**User threads** — the JVM keeps running as long as any user thread is alive.
**Daemon threads** — background threads; the JVM exits once only daemon threads remain.
**Key methods** — `sleep()`, `join()`, `yield()`, `interrupt()`.

topics.heading: Synchronization
points: **`synchronized`** — only one thread can execute that method/block on a given object at a time.
**`volatile`** — guarantees visibility across threads, but not atomicity of compound operations.
**`ReentrantLock`** — explicit lock, adds `tryLock()`, `lockInterruptibly()`, and fairness policies.
**Deadlock** — two threads each hold a lock the other needs. Prevent with consistent lock ordering or `tryLock()`.

topics.heading: Inter-Thread Communication
points: **`wait()`** — releases the object's lock and pauses until notified. Must be inside a `synchronized` block.
**`notify()`** — wakes up one waiting thread. **`notifyAll()`** — wakes up every waiting thread.
**`wait()` vs `sleep()`** — `wait()` releases the lock while paused; `sleep()` does not.
**Spurious wakeups** — always call `wait()` inside a `while` loop, not an `if`.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Consumer checks: is data ready?"] --> B{"Condition true?"}
    B -->|No| C["Consumer calls wait() - releases lock, pauses"]
    C --> D["Producer produces data"]
    D --> E["Producer calls notify()"]
    E --> F["Consumer wakes, re-acquires lock, re-checks"]
    F --> A
    B -->|Yes| G["Consumer proceeds"]
architectureFlow.caption: The consumer always re-checks its condition after waking — that's what protects against spurious wakeups.

simpleExample.scenario: Running a background task to send an order-confirmation email without blocking checkout.
simpleExample.input: A checkout flow that needs to send an email but shouldn't wait for it.
simpleExample.execution: `new Thread(...).start()` creates and starts a new thread running `sendConfirmationEmail` concurrently, while the main thread immediately continues to the next line.
simpleExample.output: `Checkout completed` often prints before `Confirmation email sent`, since the two run on independent threads with no fixed ordering.
simpleExample.explanation: Calling `.start()` (not `.run()`) is what actually creates a new thread — calling `run()` directly would just execute the lambda synchronously on the current thread.

realWorldExample.scenario: A producer thread adding orders to a queue, and a consumer thread processing them as they arrive.
realWorldExample.input: The consumer calls `takeOrder()` while the queue is empty.
realWorldExample.execution: `takeOrder()` enters the `while` loop, calls `wait()`, releasing the lock and pausing. When the producer later calls `addOrder()`, it adds the order and calls `notify()`, waking the consumer, which re-acquires the lock and re-checks the `while` condition.
realWorldExample.output: The consumer's `takeOrder()` returns the newly added order as soon as it's available, without busy-waiting in a loop.
realWorldExample.explanation: The `while (orders.isEmpty())` loop, not an `if`, protects against spurious wakeups — the consumer always re-checks the actual condition after waking up.

bestPractices: Always call `wait()` inside a `while` loop checking the actual condition, never an `if`.
Prefer higher-level `java.util.concurrent` utilities over raw `wait()`/`notify()` when possible.
Keep `synchronized` blocks as small as possible — only around the shared state being protected.
commonMistakes: Calling `run()` directly instead of `start()`, which silently runs the code on the current thread.
Calling `wait()`/`notify()` outside a `synchronized` block, causing `IllegalMonitorStateException`.
Using `if (condition) wait()` instead of `while (condition) wait()`, leaving the thread vulnerable to spurious wakeups.
interviewTips: Be ready to write the three ways to create a thread from memory, and state which is preferred and why.
If asked about deadlock, describe a concrete two-thread, two-lock example, not just the definition.

faq.q: Why is implementing Runnable preferred over extending Thread?
faq.a: Java only allows single inheritance, so extending Thread uses up a class's only opportunity to extend something. Implementing Runnable leaves that slot free, and separates "the task" from "the thread that runs it".

faq.q: What's the difference between notify() and notifyAll()?
faq.a: notify() wakes exactly one waiting thread, chosen arbitrarily. notifyAll() wakes every thread waiting on that object's monitor. notifyAll() is usually safer unless you're certain only one waiter can ever proceed correctly.

faq.q: Can a daemon thread prevent the JVM from exiting?
faq.a: No — that's the entire point of a daemon thread. The JVM shuts down once every non-daemon (user) thread has finished, regardless of whether daemon threads are still running.

summary: A thread can be created by extending Thread, implementing Runnable, or implementing Callable for a result.
A thread's lifecycle moves through NEW, RUNNABLE, RUNNING, and a waiting state, before TERMINATED.
synchronized, volatile, and ReentrantLock coordinate shared data; wait()/notify() coordinate threads on a condition.
quickRevision: Three ways to create a thread: extend Thread, implement Runnable, implement Callable.
Always call `start()`, never `run()` directly, to actually create a new thread.
Thread lifecycle: NEW → RUNNABLE → RUNNING → (BLOCKED/WAITING/TIMED_WAITING) → TERMINATED.
Daemon threads don't keep the JVM alive; user threads do.
`synchronized` guarantees visibility AND atomicity; `volatile` guarantees visibility only.
`ReentrantLock` adds `tryLock()`, `lockInterruptibly()`, and fairness over `synchronized`.
`wait()` releases the lock while paused; `sleep()` does not.
Always call `wait()` inside a `while` loop, to protect against spurious wakeups.

beginner: What are the different ways to create a thread in Java?
What is the difference between start() and run()?
What is a daemon thread?
intermediate: What's the difference between wait() and sleep()?
What's the difference between notify() and notifyAll()?
What is a deadlock, and how can it be prevented?
advanced: Why must wait() always be called inside a while loop instead of an if?
Compare synchronized and ReentrantLock in terms of the specific capabilities each one adds.
Design a simple producer-consumer system using wait()/notify(), and explain how it avoids lost wakeups.

title: Executor & ExecutorService
overview: `ExecutorService` is Java's standard way to run tasks on a managed pool of threads.
It replaces manually creating and managing individual `Thread` objects.
It also gives every task a way to report back a result or a failure.
why: A real application runs far more short-lived tasks than it has CPU cores.
Creating a brand-new OS thread for each one is wasteful and doesn't scale.
A thread pool reuses a fixed set of worker threads instead, queuing the rest.
problem: Each `Thread` maps to a real OS thread, expensive to create and real memory to hold.
Spawning one new `Thread` per incoming task, with no limit, can exhaust resources and crash the JVM.
There's also no built-in way for a raw `Thread` to hand back a result or exception to whoever started it.

intro: Creating a pool, submitting work to it, and shutting it down cleanly.

topics.heading: Why Executors
points: A thread pool reuses a fixed set of worker threads and queues excess tasks.
**`Executor`** — the simplest interface: `execute(Runnable)`, fire-and-forget.
**`ExecutorService`** extends it with lifecycle management and task tracking.

topics.heading: Creating an ExecutorService
points: **`newFixedThreadPool(n)`** — a fixed number of reusable threads.
**`newCachedThreadPool()`** — grows as needed, good for many short-lived tasks.
**`newSingleThreadExecutor()`** — one worker thread, tasks run strictly in order.
**`newScheduledThreadPool(n)`** — supports delayed/periodic tasks.
**`newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()`** (Java 21+) — one lightweight virtual thread per task.

topics.heading: Submitting & Running Tasks
points: **`execute(Runnable)`** — fire-and-forget; an uncaught exception kills that worker thread silently.
**`submit(Runnable/Callable)`** — returns a `Future`, to retrieve a result or check for exceptions.
**`invokeAll(tasks)`** — blocks until every task completes.
**`invokeAny(tasks)`** — returns the result of whichever finishes first, cancels the rest.

topics.heading: Shutdown & Lifecycle
points: **`shutdown()`** — graceful: stops accepting new tasks, lets running ones finish.
**`shutdownNow()`** — aggressive: attempts to interrupt running tasks.
**`awaitTermination(timeout, unit)`** — blocks until all tasks finish or the timeout elapses.
**Rejected tasks** — a `RejectedExecutionHandler` decides: `AbortPolicy` (default), `CallerRunsPolicy`, `DiscardPolicy`, `DiscardOldestPolicy`.

architectureFlow.caption: What actually happens to a task the moment it's submitted.

simpleExample.scenario: Running a fixed number of report-generation tasks concurrently and collecting the result.
simpleExample.input: A single report-generation task submitted to a pool of 4 worker threads.
simpleExample.execution: `submit()` hands the `Callable` to the pool. One of the 4 worker threads picks it up and runs `generateReport(merchantId)` while the calling thread continues, until `future.get()` blocks waiting for the result.
simpleExample.output: `report` holds the completed `Report` object once `generateReport()` finishes.
simpleExample.explanation: `future.get()` blocks only the calling thread, not the pool — other tasks could still be running concurrently on the other 3 worker threads the whole time.

realWorldExample.scenario: Processing incoming payment requests with a bounded thread pool, applying backpressure under heavy load.
realWorldExample.input: A burst of requests arrives faster than the pool's 8 threads and 100-slot queue can absorb.
realWorldExample.execution: Once both the pool and the queue are full, `CallerRunsPolicy` kicks in: instead of throwing or silently dropping the task, it runs the rejected task directly on the thread that called `submit()`.
realWorldExample.output: The submitting thread is temporarily forced to do the work itself, naturally slowing how fast new requests come in.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is a deliberate backpressure mechanism — it protects the service from being overwhelmed, instead of crashing or silently losing requests.

bestPractices: Always call `shutdown()` when a pool is no longer needed — an abandoned pool leaks threads.
Use a bounded queue and a sensible `RejectedExecutionHandler` instead of an unbounded queue.
Prefer `submit()` over `execute()` whenever a task's failure needs to be known.
commonMistakes: Creating a new `ExecutorService` per request instead of reusing one shared pool.
Forgetting to call `future.get()`, silently swallowing exceptions thrown inside a submitted task.
Using an unbounded queue by default and being surprised when memory grows under sustained load.
interviewTips: Know the exact difference between `shutdown()` and `shutdownNow()` — a very common follow-up question.
If asked about rejection policies, name all four and explain when `CallerRunsPolicy` specifically is useful.

faq.q: What happens to an uncaught exception in a task submitted with execute()?
faq.a: It propagates to the thread's UncaughtExceptionHandler and, by default, just gets printed to stderr — otherwise lost. With submit(), the same exception is captured inside the returned Future and re-thrown when .get() is called.

faq.q: Why would I choose newCachedThreadPool() over newFixedThreadPool()?
faq.a: newCachedThreadPool() grows and shrinks dynamically, suiting many short-lived, bursty tasks. newFixedThreadPool() caps concurrency at a known number, better protecting a system with a hard resource limit.

faq.q: What's the risk of an unbounded task queue?
faq.a: If tasks arrive faster than the pool can process them, an unbounded queue keeps growing, silently consuming memory until the application eventually runs out and crashes.

summary: ExecutorService manages a pool of reusable worker threads, instead of creating a new Thread per task.
execute() is fire-and-forget; submit() returns a Future that can carry back a result or an exception.
shutdown() drains gracefully; shutdownNow() interrupts aggressively; a RejectedExecutionHandler handles overflow.
quickRevision: ExecutorService manages a thread pool instead of raw Thread objects.
`execute(Runnable)`: fire-and-forget, no result. `submit()`: returns a `Future`.
`invokeAll()` waits for every task; `invokeAny()` returns as soon as one finishes.
Pool types: fixed, cached, single-thread, scheduled, virtual-thread-per-task (Java 21+).
`shutdown()`: graceful. `shutdownNow()`: aggressive, interrupts running tasks.
`awaitTermination()` blocks until shutdown completes or a timeout elapses.
Rejection policies: `AbortPolicy` (default), `CallerRunsPolicy`, `DiscardPolicy`, `DiscardOldestPolicy`.
An uncaught exception in `execute()` is silently lost; in `submit()`, it surfaces via `Future.get()`.

title: Future & CompletableFuture
overview: `Future` represents a result that will be available eventually, from a task submitted to an `ExecutorService`.
`CompletableFuture` extends that idea so results can be **chained, combined, and completed** without blocking.
Together they're Java's core toolkit for writing non-blocking, asynchronous code.
why: Blocking a thread just to wait for a result wastes it — the thread does nothing useful while it waits.
Real workflows often need to run several async steps in sequence or in parallel, not just one.
A composable async API lets each step trigger the next without ever calling a blocking `get()`.
problem: Plain `Future` only supports `get()`, which blocks the calling thread until the result arrives.
There's no way to say "when task A finishes, start task B" — chaining requires manually blocking and checking.
Combining two independent `Future`s requires blocking on both, one after another, instead of in parallel.

intro: Four pieces: the basic Future, CompletableFuture's core, chaining/combining, and failure handling.

topics.heading: Future
points: Returned by `executorService.submit(Callable)`. Represents a result available *eventually*.
**`get()`** — blocks until ready, or throws if the task failed. `get(timeout, unit)` avoids blocking forever.
**`isDone()` / `isCancelled()` / `cancel(mayInterrupt)`** — check or attempt to stop the task.
**Limitation** — no way to chain or combine multiple `Future`s without manually blocking on `get()`.

topics.heading: CompletableFuture Basics
points: Solves `Future`'s chaining problem — can be composed, combined, and completed without blocking.
**`supplyAsync(supplier)`** — runs a task asynchronously and returns its result.
**`runAsync(runnable)`** — same, but no return value.
Can also be completed manually: `future.complete(value)`.

topics.heading: Chaining & Combining
points: **`thenApply(fn)`** — transform the result (like `map`) once it's available.
**`thenAccept(consumer)`** — consume the result, return nothing.
**`thenCompose(fn)`** — chain to *another* `CompletableFuture` and flatten the result (like `flatMap`).
**`thenCombine(other, fn)`** — combine the results of two independent `CompletableFuture`s.
**`allOf(...)` / `anyOf(...)`** — wait for all of a group of futures, or just the first to complete.

topics.heading: Exception Handling
points: **`exceptionally(fn)`** — supply a fallback value if the pipeline threw; only runs on failure.
**`handle(fn)`** — runs regardless of outcome, receiving both result and exception (one is null).
**`whenComplete(consumer)`** — like `handle`, but only observes the outcome without transforming it.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph LR
    A["supplyAsync: fetch order"] --> B["thenApply: calculate total"]
    B --> C["thenCompose: charge payment gateway"]
    C --> D["thenAccept: send confirmation email"]
    C -->|exception| E["exceptionally: fallback/refund"]
architectureFlow.caption: A chained async pipeline — each stage hands off to the next without ever blocking.

simpleExample.scenario: Chaining a single async computation without blocking in between.
simpleExample.input: A cart ID whose total needs a discount subtracted, without blocking while the total is fetched.
simpleExample.execution: `supplyAsync()` runs `fetchCartTotal(cartId)` on a worker thread from the common pool; once it completes, `thenApply()` runs automatically on the result to subtract the discount — no manual blocking in between.
simpleExample.output: `finalTotal` is the discounted cart total, retrieved only when `get()` is finally called.
simpleExample.explanation: The calling thread is free to do other work between submitting the chain and calling `get()` — the two stages run asynchronously and automatically hand off to each other.

realWorldExample.scenario: Fetching a customer's profile and an order's pricing in parallel, then combining them into an invoice, with a fallback if pricing fails.
realWorldExample.input: A customerId and orderId, needing two independent remote calls before an invoice can be built.
realWorldExample.execution: Both `supplyAsync()` calls start immediately and run concurrently on separate threads. `thenCombine()` waits for both to complete, then merges their results into an `Invoice`. If `pricingService.calculate()` throws, `exceptionally()` supplies a fallback `Pricing` instead of failing the whole chain.
realWorldExample.output: `invoice` contains both the customer and pricing data, built without either call blocking the other.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is the pattern for any "fetch two things in parallel then merge" problem — sequential blocking calls would take the sum of both durations; this takes roughly the max of the two.

comparisonTables.title: thenApply() vs thenCompose()
rows: Use when the next step returns

bestPractices: Prefer `thenCompose()` over `thenApply()` whenever the next step itself returns a `CompletableFuture`.
Always attach `exceptionally()` or `handle()` to a chain that can fail.
Use `allOf()` to wait for a batch of independent futures instead of calling `get()` on each in a loop.
commonMistakes: Calling `get()` immediately after starting an async task, defeating the purpose of using it at all.
Using `thenApply()` when the mapping function itself returns a `CompletableFuture`, producing a nested type.
Forgetting that exceptions in a chain don't surface until a blocking call like `get()` or `join()`.
interviewTips: Be ready to explain, with an example, exactly why `thenCompose()` exists instead of always using `thenApply()`.
Know the difference between `allOf()` (waits for all) and `anyOf()` (returns after the first).

faq.q: What's the real difference between thenApply() and thenCompose()?
faq.a: thenApply() is for a plain transformation (like map) — the function returns a value. thenCompose() is for chaining to another asynchronous step (like flatMap) — the function returns another CompletableFuture, and thenCompose() flattens it instead of nesting it.

faq.q: Does CompletableFuture use the same thread pool as ExecutorService?
faq.a: By default, supplyAsync() and thenApply() run on the common ForkJoinPool, not any ExecutorService you may have created. You can pass your own Executor as an extra argument to control exactly which pool runs each stage.

faq.q: What happens if I never call get() or join() on a CompletableFuture?
faq.a: The async work still runs and completes in the background, but you'll never observe its result or any exception it threw — which is why exceptionally()/handle() should be attached even if you don't plan to block on the result.

summary: Future represents an eventual result but only supports blocking get() — no chaining or combining.
CompletableFuture adds composability: thenApply/thenAccept/thenCompose transform or chain results, thenCombine/allOf/anyOf combine multiple futures.
exceptionally(), handle(), and whenComplete() handle failures without needing a try/catch around a blocking get().
quickRevision: Future: get() blocks; isDone()/isCancelled()/cancel() manage the task; no chaining support.
CompletableFuture solves Future's chaining limitation.
supplyAsync(supplier) returns a value asynchronously; runAsync(runnable) returns nothing.
thenApply(fn): transform result (map). thenCompose(fn): chain to another CompletableFuture (flatMap).
thenAccept(consumer): consume result, no return. thenCombine(other, fn): merge two independent futures.
allOf(...): wait for all. anyOf(...): wait for the first to finish.
exceptionally(fn): fallback on failure only. handle(fn): runs on success or failure. whenComplete(): observes only.
Default execution runs on the common ForkJoinPool unless a custom Executor is passed.
